coredata.dc:title: L-tyrosine capped silver nanocluster: An efficient reusable luminescent nano-probe for simple, rapid and reliable detection of hemoglobin in real blood samples
coredata.dc:creator: P., Kavya
Aarya,
Sebastian, Anna
Sen Mojumdar, Supratik
coredata.dc:description: Easy and rapid quantification of hemoglobin (Hb) in the human body is essential for early screening of hematological disorders like anemia. Herein, we synthesized atomically precise tyrosine-protected photoluminescent silver nanocluster (Tyr-Ag NC) in a facile one-pot method. The synthesized nanocluster showed strong photoluminescence (PL) intensity peaked at 410 nm that can be selectively turned off by Hb with utmost sensitivity (limit of detection = 30 pM), thereby making the nanocluster a promising nano-probe for the detection of Hb. The observed PL quenching can be attributed to a complexation followed by a static charge transfer transition between the nanocluster and hemin, the cofactor of Hb involving both Fe2+ and the porphyrin ligand. The Fe2+ plays a key role in the complex formation which was disrupted by the formation of hydroxyl radicals through the Fe2+-H2O2 mediated Fenton reaction, restoring the PL intensity of the Tyr-Ag NC, ensuring its reusability for the detection of Hb. Compared to the conventional clinical techniques involving toxic chemicals and/or expensive equipment, the synthesized Tyr-Ag NC allows quick, direct, easy, accurate and cost-effective detection and quantification of Hb in real human serum and blood samples suggesting its tremendous practical applications for healthcare assessment.

(B) Excitation-dependent emission spectra for Tyr-Ag NC. (C) Excitation (λem = 410 nm) (violet) and emission (λex = 320 nm) (blue) spectra of Tyr-Ag NC. (D) High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of Tyr-Ag NC confirming the ultrasmall spherical structures. The inset shows the size distribution obtained from the TEM image.

(A) Emission spectra (λex = 320 nm) of Tyr-Ag NC in the absence (blue) and presence of 10 µM Hb (cyan). (B) The ratio of PL intensities (λex = 320 nm, λem = 410 nm) of Tyr-Ag NC in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of Hb (10 µM) or different other metal ions ( ∼ 50 µM) or biomolecules [glucose (

5 mM), glutathione ( ∼ 50 µM) and creatinine ( ∼ 50 µM)] or proteins [Human serum albumin (HSA) ( ∼ 100 µM), Lysozyme ( ∼ 50 µM) and Cytochrome-C ( ∼ 10 µM)].

(A) The PL spectra (λex = 320 nm) of Tyr-Ag NC in the absence (blue) and presence of 10 µM Hb (green). When the solution was treated with 100 µM of H2O2 the PL intensity of Tyr-Ag NC recovered (cyan). head.ce:title: L-tyrosine capped silver nanocluster: An efficient reusable luminescent nano-probe for simple, rapid and reliable detection of hemoglobin in real blood samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Palakkad, Kerala 678 557, India

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Palakkad, Kerala, India – 678 557

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Easy and rapid quantification of hemoglobin (Hb) in the human body is essential for early screening of hematological disorders like anemia. Herein, we synthesized atomically precise tyrosine-protected photoluminescent silver nanocluster (Tyr-Ag NC) in a facile one-pot method. The synthesized nanocluster showed strong photoluminescence (PL) intensity peaked at 410 nm that can be selectively turned off by Hb with utmost sensitivity (limit of detection = 30 pM), thereby making the nanocluster a promising nano-probe for the detection of Hb. The observed PL quenching can be attributed to a complexation followed by a static charge transfer transition between the nanocluster and hemin, the cofactor of Hb involving both Fe2+ and the porphyrin ligand. The Fe2+ plays a key role in the complex formation which was disrupted by the formation of hydroxyl radicals through the Fe2+-H2O2 mediated Fenton reaction, restoring the PL intensity of the Tyr-Ag NC, ensuring its reusability for the detection of Hb. Compared to the conventional clinical techniques involving toxic chemicals and/or expensive equipment, the synthesized Tyr-Ag NC allows quick, direct, easy, accurate and cost-effective detection and quantification of Hb in real human serum and blood samples suggesting its tremendous practical applications for healthcare assessment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hemoglobin (Hb), an iron-rich tetrameric metalloprotein present in almost all vertebrates, is the most important constituent of erythrocytes responsible for its red colour [1–3]. It plays a pivotal role in oxygen transport carrying oxygen from the respiratory organs to the tissues thereby assisting in aerobic respiration, an essential process for metabolism [2,3]. It consists of four monomeric globin subunits arranged in a tetrahedral fashion. Each monomer contains a heme group comprising one ferrous ion (Fe2+) in its reduced form coordinated to a planar organic framework named protoporphyrin-IX [2,3]. This Fe2+ can bind oxygen thereby helping hemoglobin in the oxygen transport process [2,3]. Furthermore, the amount of Fe2+ regulates the healthy hemoglobin concentration in the human body [4]. Normal hemoglobin level varies between 12 and 18 g/dL in healthy humans (adults) [5,6]. Low concentration of healthy hemoglobin in the blood leads to the most common hematological disorder anemia, in which insufficient oxygen is being transported to the tissues and organs due to lack of oxygen carrier, severely inhibiting the metabolism process leading to fatigue and weakness [7–9]. Anemia is a serious health condition affecting one in five people worldwide, particularly some vulnerable sections including adolescent girls, pregnant women, and preschool children [7–9]. It can affect the overall motor development in children if remains untreated [7,8]. Irregularity in the structure and concentration of Hb is linked to several other health disorders like pulmonary fibrosis [10], congenital heart disease [11], heart failure [12], chronic kidney disease [12], strokes [13], erythrocytosis [14], thalassemia [15], etc. Thus, periodic monitoring of hemoglobin levels accurately is extremely critical for the diagnosis and real-time monitoring of these diseases.

Thus far many analytical methods have been exploited for the detection of hemoglobin, namely colorimetric assays [16], automated hematology analysis [17], electrochemical analysis [18], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [19], mass spectrometry [20], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [21]. The colorimetric method involving cyanmethemoglobin is regarded as the gold standard for Hb detection and is frequently employed for clinical examinations as it is more accurate and economical [16]. However, the method suffers from some drawbacks such as, it is time-consuming, tedious and involves the use of a potentially lethal reagent, potassium cyanide. It also demands expensive laboratory equipment and clinical experts for operation and data interpretation. The other commonly used colorimetric method involves toxic sodium azide [22]. The automated hematology analyzer is a medical laboratory instrument to perform complete blood counts using both electrical and optical techniques. Unfortunately, the instrument requires high maintenance, frequent calibration, a stable climate and a trained technician to operate it [23]. The electrochemical sensing method requires sample preparation and electrode surface modification which are time-consuming and may also introduce errors in results [18]. Other available Hb detection methods like HPLC, and ELISA also require cumbersome sample preparation, sophisticated instrumentation facilities, trained personnel, and high cost for operation [24]. Any method to be considered quintessential for screening of Hb must meet certain criteria including being reliable, rapid, easy to use, affordable, portable, reagent-free, pre-analytical error-proof and user-friendly. Unfortunately, none of the currently available Hb screening techniques satisfies these aforementioned requirements [23], thus necessitating an improved, facile, environment- and user-friendly method for the easy and rapid detection of Hb.

Fluorescence-based methods such as quantum dots [24,25], nanoparticles [26], polymers [27,28], macrocycles [29] and metal-organic frameworks [30,31] have gained a lot of recent attention due to their easy, rapid, selective, and sensitive detection ability. A slight variation in the analyte concentration around the probe can lead to a substantial change in the fluorescence intensity leading to a lower detection limit, thereby making them superior to other methods. Ligand scaffolded metal nanoclusters emerged as a new generation nanoprobe and are of prime interest due to their excellent optical and electronic properties [32–36], playing a seminal role among the different luminescent detection methods. Metal nanoclusters (MNCs) are conglomerates of a few to tens of metal atoms with a size of ∼ 2 nm [32–36]. The surface ligand effects and the quantum confinement phenomenon provide superior luminescence properties to metal nanoclusters over other fluorescent nanoprobes [32–36]. They are preferred over other fluorescence probes due to their tuneable emission characteristics, photostability, biocompatibility, cost-effective synthesis, and simplicity to incorporate into various sensing platforms, resulting in the accurate, selective, and sensitive detection of toxic metal ions [37,38], biomolecules [39,40], antibiotics [41] etc. So far a limited number of fluorescence-based methods have been explored for the detection of Hb. But they too suffer from one or more drawbacks, such as indirect detection method leading to more erroneous results, cytotoxicity to human health, time-consuming extraction of Hb, pretreatment of Hb before detection, and relatively higher detection limit [42–50]. This is thus a timely opportunity to develop a novel nanocluster-based cost-effective photoluminescence (PL)-based method for the rapid and easy detection of Hb.

Among noble metal nanoclusters, Ag nanoclusters [33,39,51–53] are particularly of interest because of their strong photoluminescence properties, improved stability, a wide range of applicability, anti-microbial property and most importantly low toxicity compared to other quantum analogues such as quantum dots [54]. Silver has a strong affinity towards Hb [55–57] and thus could be an ideal candidate for the PL-based method for the detection of Hb. Scaffolding agents play an important role in tuning the selectivity of the nanoclusters towards specific analytes [33,39,58]. Thus choosing the correct scaffolding agent is critical for the design of the detection methods for specific analytes. Among different ligands, proteins have been widely used as a stabilizing agent for the synthesis of nanoclusters due to the presence of a large number of functional groups in the side chains of the amino acids that can bind and stabilize the metal core easily [33,35,39,53,59,60]. In proteins, tyrosine specifically plays a major role in reducing as well as stabilizing the metal core [61,62], thus expected to stabilize the nanocluster alone without involving the relatively bigger polymeric structure. Concurrently, a smaller scaffold may avoid any undue interactions among them or with the surroundings. Tyrosine has previously been used as a scaffolding agent for the synthesis of nanoclusters [62–64] and thus could be an ideal choice for the current study.

Herein, we successfully engineered a stable, luminescent, ultra-small L-tyrosine-protected silver nanocluster (Tyr-Ag NC) by adopting a facile, one-pot, and green protocol. The synthesized Tyr-Ag NC proved to selectively respond towards Hb. The long strong photoluminescence of the nanocluster was systematically suppressed by the gradual addition of Hb through a static charge transfer mechanism with an ultra-low detection limit of 30 pM. Astonishingly, the quenched photoluminescence intensity can be regained by simply adding hydrogen peroxide to the mixture, making the nanocluster reusable. The detailed mechanisms were explored in this work. This nanoprobe thus can be successfully used for the simple, cost-effective, rapid and accurate detection of Hb in real blood samples and replace the conventional methods. The peak ability of Tyr-Ag NC can further be exploited for the point-of-care Hb diagnosis by integrating this miraculous nanomaterial into a portable sensor.
For materials and instrumentation, please refer to the supporting information.

Tyrosine-protected Ag nanocluster was prepared under ambient and aqueous conditions modifying the previously reported method [64] to achieve an improved photoluminescent quantum yield, smaller size, better stability and faster reaction time. In this typical optimized procedure, 2 mL of 3 mM aqueous solution of L-tyrosine was mixed with 1 mL of 0.6 mM AgNO3 solution in a molar ratio of 1:10 (Ag:Tyr) followed by drop-wise addition of 1 M 100 µL NaOH solution to adjust the pH of the solution around 11. The mixture was thoroughly stirred at 40 °C and the PL intensity was successively measured after each hour. The PL intensity of the reaction mixture gradually increased up to 6 hr and then steadily decreased, suggesting an optimized reaction time of ∼ 6 hr. The mixture was then centrifuged to remove larger particles and the resulting supernatant was collected and stored at 4 °C under dark conditions for further use.

For the selectivity study, desired concentrations of different metal ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+), biomolecules (glucose, glutathione, creatinine, amino acids, hemoglobin, hemin chloride, protoporphyrin-IX) and proteins (human serum albumin, lysozyme, Cytochrome C) were added to the 5-fold diluted Tyr-Ag NC solution and incubated for 3 mins. After incubation, the photoluminescence intensity (F) was recorded and compared to the intensity of the nanocluster without any analyte (F0). To test the sensitivity, incremental concentrations of Hb (from 10 pM to 10 µM) were added to 2 mL diluted NC solution. Emission spectra were recorded after 3 min incubation for each addition. All the measurements were done under ambient conditions. All the errors reported in this study represent the standard error of the mean (s.e.m.) calculated from at least three or more independent measurements (n ≥ 3). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated based on the equation: LOD = 3σ/K, where σ is the standard deviation and K is the slope of the linear curve [35,65].

For the reusability study, in each cycle, the PL intensity of the 2 mL diluted Tyr-Ag NC solution was first quenched by 10 μM hemoglobin followed by the addition of 2 µL of 100 mM H2O2. PL emission spectra were recorded after each step. The mixture was loaded in a 0.5 mL 10 kDa cut-off centrifugal filter and subjected to centrifugation at 14000×g for 7 min. The filtrate was collected for further analysis. This process of adding 10 µM Hb and spin filtration cycle was repeated for 4 times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hb in human serum and real blood samples
ce:section.ce:para: The purchased human serum was diluted by 100-fold and 100 µL of this diluted serum sample was added to 900 µL of the diluted nanocluster. Emission spectra were taken before and after the addition of serum to quantify the Hb present in the serum. Then different concentrations of Hb (100 pM, 100 nM, 500 nM and 2000 nM) were spiked into the standard solution containing nanocluster and serum and emission spectra were recorded on each addition.

Human blood samples were obtained from three volunteer donors. 10 µL of blood samples were collected by finger pricking method using sterilized needles. Blood samples were then diluted 10-fold with milli-Q water. Subsequently, 10 µL of this diluted blood solution was mixed with 990 µL of NC solution. The PL intensity of the nanocluster solution was measured in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of the blood samples. From the F0/F ratio, the amount of Hb in the real blood samples was estimated using the calibration curve: F0/F = 1.08 + 3.66 × 105 [Hb], where [Hb] is the concentration of Hb in mole/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tyr-Ag NC: Photophysical and structural properties
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescence intensity of the reaction mixture increased significantly upon the formation of the Ag nanoclusters. The enhanced PL intensity of the tyrosine capped Ag nanocluster (Tyr-Ag NC) compared to the pure tyrosine stirred under the same reaction condition, strongly corroborates that the PL properties are intrinsic exclusively to the metal core rather than the tyrosine scaffold ( Fig. 1A). The excitation-dependent emission spectra suggest that the maximum emission intensity can be achieved by exciting the NC at 320 nm (Fig. 1B). This observation was further well supported by the excitation spectra (λem = 410 nm) (Fig. 1C) denoting that when excited at 320 nm, the Tyr-Ag NC exhibited a strong blue PL intensity (λem = 410 nm) with a substantial photoluminescence quantum yield (ϕ = 0.052). Interestingly, unlike several other nanoclusters [34,66], Tyr-Ag NC didn’t show any shift in the emission maxima (410 nm) upon varying the excitation wavelength (Fig. 1B) distinctly indicating that the nanoclusters were well dispersed with uniform size [39,67,68] which further verified by the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) measurements. TEM image established the monodisperse ultrasmall size (2.5 ± 0.3 nm) of the Tyr-Ag NC (Fig. 1D). The PL intensity thus can be attributed to the quantum size of the nanocluster.

The amine and carboxyl groups of tyrosine engage electrostatically with Ag+ ions, resulting in the initiation of nanocluster formation [64,69]. Under alkaline conditions, the ionized phenolic group of tyrosine reduces Ag+ to Ag0 [61,62,64], which further promotes the nanocluster formation through coalescence [33,38,39]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirms the zero oxidation state of Ag. The peaks at around 368 eV and 374 eV correspond to 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 respectively suggesting the existence of Ag0 [70]. Since the binding energy peaks for Ag+ appear just 0.5 eV apart from that of Ag0, the existence of a trace amount of Ag+ cannot be ruled out (Fig. S1) [70]. The most intense m/z peak around 505 observed in the matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight (MALDI-TOF) spectrum may be assigned to the most probable fragment composition of Tyr1Ag3 while the bigger fragments result in the larger m/z values (Fig. S2). The Tyr-Ag NC exhibited exceptional photostability, maintaining their PL properties without degradation for over 3 months when stored at 4 °C under dark conditions (Fig. S3). The lack of any effect on the PL intensity of Tyr-Ag NC when exposed to a strong oxidizing agent like H2O2 indicates that the synthesized nanocluster exhibits remarkable stability against oxidative damage (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tyr-Ag NC: Selective and sensitive detection of hemoglobin (Hb)
ce:section.ce:para: Since the emission spectrum of Tyr-Ag NC (λem = 410 nm) has a significant overlap with the absorption spectra of Hemoglobin (Hb) (λmax = 405 nm) [55], an interaction between the two was expected. Indeed, as expected, the strong photoluminescence intensity of the Tyr-Ag NC was significantly quenched (∼ 82%) instantaneously upon the direct addition of a small concentration (∼10 μM) of Hb ( Fig. 2 A). Based on this remarkable result, a turn-off-based fluorescence assay was developed for the rapid detection of Hb. Since Hb exists in a complex blood matrix, to check the selectivity of Tyr-Ag NC towards Hb, the photoluminescence responses of the nanocluster were measured in the presence of the coexisting constituents [71]. Quenching efficiencies for a series of metal ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+), biomolecules (glucose, glutathione, creatinine, amino acids) and proteins (Human serum albumin (HSA), Lysozyme, Cytochrome-C) commonly constituting the major part of the human blood were examined. None of them render any considerable change in the PL intensity of the Tyr-Ag NC compared to that by Hb (10 μM) strongly demonstrates its selectivity towards Hb (Fig. 2B and S5). It is worthwhile to mention that among all the screened constituents, 10 μM Cytochrome C (same concentration as that of Hb) could quench the PL intensity of the nanocluster to some extent (quenching efficiency ∼40%; F0/F = 1.67). As its concentration in real blood samples is extremely low (∼10−6 times compared to that of Hb), Cytochrome C should not interfere in the detection of Hb in real blood samples. The interference study further confirmed that the selectivity and detection efficiency of the Tyr-Ag NC towards Hb remains unaltered even in the presence of high concentrations (typical concentrations found in human blood) of common blood constituents as interferers in the medium (Fig. S6).

Since the proteinogenic amino acids did not change the PL intensity of the Tyr-Ag NC appreciably (Fig. S5), one can assume that the peptide chain (globin part) of Hb does not play any key role in the PL turn-off mechanism. On the contrary, both Hb and Cytochrome C can reasonably quench the PL intensity of the Tyr-Ag NC, suggesting that there must be some common factor. Upon closer examination, it was evident that the hemin unit (porphyrin ring containing iron) present in both proteins plays a critical role in the quenching mechanism. Since Hb is a tetrameric protein (each unit of Hb contains 4 units of hemin), it would be reasonable to compare 10 μM Hb with 40 μM hemin. As a matter of fact, 40 μM hemin resulted in an 83% (F0/F = 5.8) reduction in the PL intensity of the Tyr-Ag NC, precisely matching with that by 10 μM Hb (quenching efficiency ∼82%; F0/F = 5.4) confirming its role in the PL turn-off process ( Fig. 3). It was further revealed that the metal cofactors (Fe2+/Fe3+) alone were unable to impart any significant change in the PL intensity of the Tyr-Ag NC. Moreover, only the porphyrin ring (protoporphyrin IX) was also not enough to account for the quenching efficiency exhibited by Hb, rather a cooperative mechanism involving both of them (metal and porphyrin ring together as a hemin unit) was crucial (Fig. 3).

Earlier, the inner filter effect, an experimental artifact, has been considered as a source of fluorescence quenching for many Hb detection methods [44–46]. In the present study, the inner filter effect as a reason for the PL quenching was disregarded because this phenomenon is more prominent at the higher concentration of the analyte rather than in a diluted solution in the ultra-low concentration regime (pM-nM) [72]. Furthermore, Hb absorbs more strongly at neutral pH compared to that of basic pH but less quenching efficiency can be observed for Hb under neutral pH further excluding the inner filter effect as a reason (Fig. S7). Commonly, H2O2-mediated quenching of PL intensity of the fluorophores seems to play a major role in Hb sensing. H2O2 reacts with Fe2+ (Fenton reaction) of hemoglobin thereby generating hydroxyl radicals which readily quenches the PL intensity of the fluorophores [42–45,47,50]. But, this indirect mechanism is also not valid in the present study, since the Tyr-Ag NC is extremely stable in the presence of hydroxyl radicals (Fig. S8).

Silver ions and nanoparticles were shown to have an affinity towards Hb, forming silver-Hb bioconjugate. This interaction involves a conformational transition of Hb as well as a redox-driven electron transfer process between the protein and the metal core [55,56]. A similar interaction between Ag or Au nanoparticles and Hb has also been reported earlier [57]. Recent studies further confirmed a strong binding affinity of silver ions specifically towards N atoms, thus suggesting a possible complexation between Ag and the N-atoms of the hemin, the prosthetic group of Hb [73]. Hemin can further quench the PL intensity of metal nanoclusters through complexation followed by charge transfer transition [74]. In the present study, the PL spectrum of Tyr-Ag NC transitioned from a single peak to a dual peak in the presence of a higher concentration of Hb, indicating the possibility of the formation of a Tyr-Ag NC-Hb complex (Fig. 2 A) [75]. The red-shifted peak around 430 nm can be assigned to the NC-Hemin interaction (Fig. S9) while the blue-shifted peak around 380 nm may be ascribed to the NC surrounded by the hydrophobic regions of the protein.

The complexation was further confirmed by DLS and HRTEM studies (Fig. S10). The DLS spectrum for the Tyr-Ag NC in the presence of Hb clearly exhibited two distinct peaks around 5.4 nm and 210 nm corresponding to the pure nanocluster and the NC-Hb complex respectively (Fig. S10A). From the peaks it was evident that the population of the pure nanoclusters was much less than that of the NC-Hb complex at the higher (∼10 μM) Hb concentration. HRTEM image further supported the notion of complex formation. TEM image confirmed that the size of the Tyr-Ag NC increased by ∼3 fold (from 2.5 ± 0.3 nm in the absence of Hb to 7.5 ± 0.8 nm in the presence of 10 μM Hb) upon the addition of Hb suggesting the formation NC-Hb complex (Fig. S10B). It is important to note that, TEM captures the true size of the particle in the dry state (i.e. most compact state) while DLS measures the size of the solvated particle with an ionic layer around it (hydration sphere diameter), thus DLS typically shows a higher particle size compared to TEM [53,76]. Further aggregation can also be possible in the solution phase. This justifies the size variation between TEM and DLS measurements. Based on these results, a ground-state complex formation followed by charge transfer between the Tyr-Ag NC and the hemin moiety of Hb could thus be the best possible justification that can explain the experimental observations [50,55–57,74].

Furthermore, pH-dependent quenching studies also agree with this picture (Fig. S7B). Under basic medium (pH∼11), the Hb partially unfolds allowing the hemin to interact and form a complex easily with the Tyr-Ag NC showing a higher quenching efficiency (F0/F = 5.4). The circular dichroism (CD) spectra of Hb in the absence and presence of the nanocluster further indicate a conformational change of Hb during the formation of the NC-Hb complex (Fig. S11). Under neutral conditions (pH∼7), the hemin moiety is relatively less accessible and hidden within the natively folded protein structure, exhibiting relatively lower quenching efficiency (F0/F = 4.0). Under acidic pH (pH∼3), the hemin group completely detached from the Hb [77,78]. The nitrogen of the hemin group gets protonated and can neither coordinate with the protein, nor with the nanocluster, showing even lower quenching efficiency (F0/F = 2.5). These results thus suggest that the interaction between the Ag atoms of the NC and the N atoms of the hemin unit are critical for the Tyr Ag NC-Hb complex formation.

To assess the sensitivity of the Tyr-Ag NC, an increasing concentration of Hb solution (from 10 pM to 10 µM) was systemically added to the nanocluster thereupon gradually decreasing the PL intensity of the nanocluster ( Fig. 4 A). The intensity-based Stern-Volmer plot (F0/F vs Hb concentration, where F0 and F represent the PL intensities in the absence and presence of the Hb respectively) exhibited a linear relationship with two distinct slopes suggesting a complex quenching mechanism. Initially, at a lower Hb concentration (up to ∼ 200 pM) a rapid quenching response can be observed (KSV = 4 ×108 M−1) followed by a fairly slower response (KSV = 3.66 ×105 M−1) at a relatively higher concentration (> 200 pM) (Fig. 4B). These findings point towards the existence of two distinct kinetic processes governing the observed phenomenon which cannot be solely accounted for by diffusion-controlled quenching [35,79]. The detailed examination of the excited state PL lifetime of the nanocluster in the presence of Hb further provides valuable insights into the quenching mechanism. The average PL lifetime of the Tyr-Ag NC was estimated to be 4.6 ns in the absence of Hb (Fig. S12A). Upon incremental addition of Hb, no substantial alteration in the PL lifetime was observed, supporting the notion of a purely static quenching mechanism where the fluorophore interacts and forms a non-fluorescent complex with the quencher at the ground state (Fig. S12B) [34,35]. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 30 pM, significantly lower than the other Hb detection methods (Table S1).

To gain additional key mechanistic insights, we checked the effect of the redox environment on the quenching process. In the presence of a reducing agent hydrazine (N2H4), the quenching efficiency of Hb remained unaffected (F0/F = 5.1) (Fig. S13). Earlier report showed that under a reducing environment, the conversion of Fe2+ to Fe3+ was inhibited [60], thus strongly suggesting that the Fe2+ to Fe3+ conversion is not essential for the quenching process. In contrast, an oxidizing agent (H2O2) can significantly reduce the quenching efficiency (F0/F = 1.6) (Fig. S13), further implying that, although Fe2+ to Fe3+ transition is not important, Fe2+ plays a key role in the quenching process by helping in the complex formation. In the presence of H2O2, this complex formation is disfavoured, as a result, quenching efficiency is decreased.

Based on these intriguing results, we designed a method to recycle the Tyr-Ag NC. Upon the addition of H2O2 (100 μM) to the nanocluster previously quenched by Hb, as expected the PL intensity of the nanocluster was rapidly restored ( Fig. 5A). This remarkable observation may be explained by the in-situ generation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) through the Fe2+-catalyzed Fenton reaction. The hydroxyl radicals can cleave the quencher molecules from the nanocluster breaking the complex and leading to a dramatic increase in the fluorescence intensity [80]. Subsequently, the oxidized and cleaved Hb can be removed from the Tyr-Ag NC solution mixture through centrifugation using a centrifugal filter. This purified Tyr-Ag NC can be further reused for the detection of Hb and this process can be repeated for several cycles (Fig. 5B). This decomplexation was well supported by the TEM images. Upon the addition of H2O2 to the solution mixture containing NC-Hb complex, the size remarkably dropped back from 7.5 ± 0.8 nm to 3.2 ± 0.6 nm (Fig. S10B and S14). This average diameter of 3.2 ± 0.6 nm (Fig. S14) matched nicely to the initial size of the nanocluster (2.5 ± 0.3 nm), clearly indicating the decomplexation. The Tyr-Ag NC thus can be used as an extremely robust, cost-effective, reusable method for the sensitive and selective detection of Hb.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of Hb in human serum and real blood samples
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the ultra-low detection limit (30 pM) of Tyr-Ag NC we extended our study to quantify the amount of Hb present in the human serum sample. Monitoring of low abundance of Hb in human serum is challenging. In healthy individuals, a small amount of hemoglobin can be found in the serum due to the natural breakdown of RBC. Elevated levels of Hb in the serum can be an indicator of medical conditions like hemolytic anemia or other disorders affecting RBC. The quantity of Hb present in the 1000-fold diluted human serum was calculated to be 3.02 nM, which corresponds to the stock concentration of 3.02 μM (i.e. 19.5 mg/dL) which was well within the reported range of Hb in the product catalogue (≤ 30 mg/dL). To check the reliability of our Tyr-Ag NC-based method, we further spiked the complex serum sample with known concentrations of Hb and thereafter estimated the concentration recovered experimentally using the nanocluster ( Table 1). The results listed in Table 1 clearly indicate that the Tyr-Ag NC-based method can be reliably used for the detection and recovery of Hb in complex systems.

The exceptional performance of the Tyr-Ag NC-based luminescent probe indicates its strong potential for the rapid quantification of Hb in real human blood samples. Considering the concentration range of Hb in adult blood typically lies between 12 and 18 g/dL, the blood samples were appropriately diluted to minimize any potential interferences. This dilution was made possible only due to the high sensitivity and low detection limit of Tyr-Ag NC. The Hb levels in the finger blood of three volunteer donors were quantified using the proposed luminescent probe ( Table 2). A tiny amount (1–2 µL) of blood sample is required for this process which can be added after appropriate dilution directly to the Tyr-Ag NC solution without any pre-treatment. The amount of Hb detected in the real blood samples experimentally using the Tyr-Ag NC-based method agreed exceptionally well (∼100%) with the result obtained from the pathology lab using the standard cyanmethemoglobin method, reinforcing the reliability and accuracy of the proposed method. These compelling results thus endorse Tyr-Ag NC as an excellent nano-probe for the rapid, reliable and cost-effective quantification of Hb in real samples. With a direct and precise approach and incredible accuracy, this nano-probe has the potential to provide a simple alternative method for the clinical quantification of Hb.

In conclusion, we have successfully synthesized a stable, photoluminescent Tyr-Ag NC using tyrosine both as the template and reductant through a straightforward one–pot aqueous method under ambient conditions. This nanocluster displayed remarkable photoluminescence properties with peak emission at 410 nm upon excitation at 320 nm. The PL intensity was drastically quenched upon exposing the nanocluster to a medium containing Hb. Thus, a turn-off-based PL assay has been developed for the easy, rapid, selective and sensitive detection of Hb even at the picomolar concentration range. The PL quenching proceeds via a ground state complex formation followed by charge transfer between the heme moiety of the hemoglobin and the photoluminescent Tyr-Ag NC. Upon treatment with H2O2, this complex formation was hindered due to the formation of hydroxyl radicals through Fe2+-mediated Fenton reaction, allowing the Tyr-Ag NC to regain the lost (by Hb) PL intensity making it a cost-effective reusable method. Further, its applicability has been tested in real samples. Its performance of quantifying Hb in real human serum and blood samples was exceptional. The ease of operation and swiftness of the PL-based Hb detection method enable it as an alternative to the conventional clinical Hb detection approach followed in the pathological laboratory. These findings highlight its promising application in rapid, reliable and accurate clinical quantification of Hb at the point of care, opening the gateway for shaping portable Hb-sensor with precision and efficiency.

Kavya P.: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Aarya: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Anna Sebastian: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Supratik Sen Mojumdar: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ms. Kavya P. joined the Department of Chemistry, IIT Palakkad in Aug 2022. Currently she is working as a PhD scholar under the supervision of Dr. Supratik Sen Mojumdar.
Ms. Aarya joined the Department of Chemistry, IIT Palakkad in Jan 2021. Currently she is working as a PhD scholar under the supervision of Dr. Supratik Sen Mojumdar.
Ms. Anna Sebastian joined the Department of Chemistry, IIT Palakkad in Jan 2020. Currently she is working as a PhD scholar under the supervision of Dr. Supratik Sen Mojumdar.

Dr. Supratik Sen Mojumdar received his PhD degree from IACS, Kolkata in 2013. He carried out his post-doctoral research at the University of Alberta, Canada from 2013 to 2019. He joined the Department of Chemistry, IIT Palakkad in June 2019 and presently working as an Associate Professor. His research interests include luminescent metal nanoclusters, protein folding/misfolding dynamics using single molecule spectroscopy.
